one_line_takeaway: AWE shows that a specialized, memory-augmented multi-agent architecture with domain-specific exploitation pipelines and browser-based verification can beat a stronger general-purpose LLM pentesting agent (MAPTA/GPT-5) on injection-class vulnerabilities (e.g., 87% vs 57% XSS solve rate) using a weaker model (Claude Sonnet 4) at roughly 98% fewer tokens and ~4x lower cost, at the expense of much lower overall XBOW coverage (51.9% vs 76.9%) on tasks needing multi-step/business-logic reasoning.
key_finding: On the XBOW benchmark, MAPTA achieves a higher overall solve rate (76.9%, 80/104) than AWE (51.9%, 54/104), reflecting MAPTA's broader unrestricted-sandbox exploratory reach. However, AWE is far more efficient: average solve time 53.1s vs MAPTA's 190.8s (median 35.7s vs 156.2s, a 4-5x speedup across percentiles); total token usage 1.12M vs MAPTA's 54.87M (~98% reduction, 20.7K vs 685.9K tokens/solve); total API cost $7.73 vs $21.38 (cost/solve $0.113 vs $0.267) -- despite AWE using a mid-tier model (Claude Sonnet 4) against MAPTA's GPT-5. Per-category (Table III, injection classes): XSS -- AWE 20/23 (87%) vs MAPTA 13/23 (57%); Blind SQLi -- AWE 2/3 (67%) vs MAPTA 1/3 (33%); SQLi, XXE, and SSRF -- both 100% (6/6, 3/3, 3/3 respectively); SSTI -- AWE 7/13 (54%) vs MAPTA 11/13 (85%); Command Injection -- AWE 5/11 (45%) vs MAPTA 9/11 (82%). AWE failed 50/104 challenges (about one-third intentionally out of scope such as business logic/deserialization/crypto misuse, about a quarter needing multi-step stateful reasoning AWE's agents cannot express); MAPTA failed 24/104; both failed on 15. In the DVWA model-selection study, all three models hit 100% on reflected XSS; for CSP-enforced stored XSS, Claude Sonnet 4 and GPT-4o tied at 67% while Gemini 2.0 Flash reached 50%; for blind SQLi, Claude reached 70%, GPT-4o 60%, Gemini 55%; Claude converged in 10-40 payload attempts versus ~20% more for GPT-4o and ~40% more for Gemini.
core_contribution: The paper introduces AWE (Adaptive Web Exploitation), a memory-augmented, multi-agent autonomous web penetration testing system. Unlike general-purpose LLM pentesting agents that rely on unconstrained exploration, AWE embeds structured, vulnerability-specific analysis pipelines (specialized agents for XSS, SQLi, SSTI, command injection, XXE, SSRF, IDOR, LFI) inside a lightweight LLM orchestration layer. Its architecture has three layers: an Orchestration Layer (global exploitation state, LLM-driven agent selection/planning, resource budgeting), a Specialized Agents Layer (per-vulnerability-class exploitation modules that encode expert methodology, e.g. a five-phase XSS pipeline: canary injection to map reflection, DOM-context disambiguation, filter-policy probing, LLM-synthesized context-tailored payloads, and definitive verification), and a Foundation Layer (persistent short-/long-term memory of tried payloads/filters/bypasses, a headless-Chromium Browser Verification Engine for ground-truth confirmation of execution/DOM mutation, plus endpoint discovery and fingerprinting). The design philosophy is: specialization over generalized reasoning, stateful/memory-driven operation, and verification (concrete evidence) rather than speculative reporting of vulnerabilities.
threat_model: Stated explicitly in Section II. Black-box automated attacker interacting with modern web apps solely through HTTP GET/POST requests against parameterized endpoints (PHP/Python/Node.js/Java backends), with no source-code access, runtime logs, or internal state -- observations come only from HTTP responses and timing. The attacker may use benign registration/low-privilege credentials for authenticated probing, uses commercial LLM APIs (cost-bounded) to synthesize context-aware payloads, and operates under a strict per-endpoint time budget (≤10 minutes). Target application stack and hosting infrastructure are assumed uncompromised/trustworthy; all attacker-controlled inputs (parameters, headers, cookies, bodies) are treated as the exploitation surface. Scope is explicitly limited to injection-centric vulnerabilities discoverable via black-box input manipulation (XSS, SQLi in various forms, SSTI, command injection, LFI/path traversal, XXE, SSRF, and IDOR with valid credentials); explicitly out of scope are network/protocol-level attacks, cryptographic weaknesses, and business-logic flaws requiring semantic/multi-step reasoning beyond observable request-response behavior.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations (Section VIII): (1) Scope restrictions -- targets only injection-centric vulnerability classes, explicitly excludes reasoning-heavy categories (business logic, complex auth workflows, protocol-level issues like request smuggling/desync). (2) Limited multi-step planning -- agents run largely independent pipelines with no coordination across multi-stage exploitation sequences (e.g., default-credential discovery -> IDOR -> privilege escalation is out of reach). (3) Reliance on heuristic abstractions -- context/filter models encode assumptions about server behavior and sanitization that may fail against highly idiosyncratic frameworks or obfuscated sinks. (4) LLM sensitivity -- Claude Sonnet 4 performed best in their tests, but model-dependent variability and pricing shifts remain a systemic risk to long-term stability. Additionally noted by the paper itself: MAPTA's XBOW results were not independently reproduced but taken from MAPTA's own published per-challenge numbers, and AWE trades solve-rate breadth (it loses to MAPTA on SSTI and command injection, and overall) for large efficiency/cost gains on the injection classes it targets.

datasets_used: XBOW benchmark (104 containerized web challenges spanning 26 vulnerability categories, hidden-flag exploit-based scoring)
DVWA (Damn Vulnerable Web Application) -- reflected XSS, stored XSS (CSP-enforced), DOM-based XSS, error-based SQLi, time-based blind SQLi, n=10 trials per vulnerability type
quantitative_results: Overall XBOW solve rate: AWE 51.9% (54/104) vs MAPTA 76.9% (80/104)
Avg. solve time: AWE 53.1s vs MAPTA 190.8s; median 35.7s vs 156.2s (4-5x speedup)
Total token usage: AWE 1.12M vs MAPTA 54.87M (~98% reduction); tokens/solve 20.7K vs 685.9K
Total API cost: AWE $7.73 vs MAPTA $21.38; cost/solve $0.113 vs $0.267

Per-category solve counts (AWE vs MAPTA out of total): XSS 20/23 (87%) vs 13/23 (57%); Blind SQLi 2/3 (67%) vs 1/3 (33%); SQLi 6/6 (100%) vs 6/6 (100%); XXE 3/3 (100%) vs 3/3 (100%); SSRF 3/3 (100%) vs 3/3 (100%); SSTI 7/13 (54%) vs 11/13 (85%); Command Injection 5/11 (45%) vs 9/11 (82%)
Failure counts: AWE failed 50/104, MAPTA failed 24/104, both failed 15

DVWA model comparison: reflected XSS 100% for all 3 models (Claude Sonnet 4, GPT-4o, Gemini 2.0 Flash); CSP-enforced stored XSS Claude 67% = GPT-4o 67% > Gemini 50%; blind SQLi Claude 70% > GPT-4o 60% > Gemini 55%; payload-convergence iterations Claude 10-40, GPT-4o ~20% more, Gemini ~40% more
baselines_compared: MAPTA (David & Gervais, arXiv:2508.20816) -- three-role multi-agent (Coordinator/Sandbox/Validation) autonomous pentesting system using GPT-5 in extended-reasoning mode with an unrestricted Docker sandbox; results taken from MAPTA's own published per-challenge XBOW numbers, not re-run by the authors
GPT-4o and Gemini 2.0 Flash as alternate backbone LLMs within AWE's own architecture (DVWA ablation, not external baselines)
